bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Anthropic as fighting punitive government overreach, stressing words like "blacklisting" and "unlawful campaign of retaliation" and praising its ethical "safety guardrails" against mass surveillance and autonomous weapons; right-leaning coverage frames the Pentagon move as prudent national-security caution, invoking "supply chain risk," "national security threat," and pejoratives like "woke" to paint the company as politically motivated and obstructionist.
Center outlets emphasize legal procedure and precedent.

Pivotal divides—whether the designation is retaliation versus justified security and claims of battlefield use—spark the sharpest partisan splits, though all agree the litigation tests AI–military norms.
summary: On Monday, Anthropic filed two lawsuits in California and Washington, D.C., challenging the Pentagon's supply‑chain‑risk designation and seeking to block enforcement.

Months‑long public disagreements over guardrails for autonomous weapons and domestic surveillance set the stage for legal action as Anthropic drew firm red lines against mass surveillance and fully autonomous weapons.
More than 500 customers pay at least $1 million annually for Claude, and Anthropic says the designation jeopardizes hundreds of millions and its projected $14 billion in revenue this year.

The Pentagon last week formally designated Anthropic a supply‑chain risk and informed the company it was banned from defense work, effective immediately, while President Donald Trump ordered federal agencies to stop using Claude with a six‑month phase‑out prompting some defense contractors to pause work.

Claiming retaliation, Anthropic invoked the Constitution and urged judicial review, stating 'The Constitution does not allow ​the government to wield its enormous power to punish a company for its protected speech' and noting this is the first known use against a U.S. firm.
title: Source Icon

bias: Left coverage emphasizes the anti‑Muslim organizer as a "far‑right agitator" and frames the incident as escalation "rooted in bigotry and racism," while right outlets foreground Islamist motive—labeling suspects " ISIS fanatics" and highlighting "Allahu Akbar," "Mother of Satan," and the "FBI" "terror probe" to allege officials "refused to condemn.
Left's tone censures organizers; right uses law‑and‑order alarm and moral indictment of the mayor/media.
Pivotal divides are whether to label it terrorism versus awaiting forensic/legal proof; center coverage de‑emphasized political critiques.
All sides agree an IED was used, two suspects were arrested, and federal investigators are involved.
summary: On Saturday, improvised explosive devices were hurled at an anti-Islam demonstration outside Gracie Mansion, and authorities are investigating as ISIS -inspired, with two suspects in custody.
Investigators say Emir Balat and Ibrahim Kayumi traveled from Pennsylvania to 'bring violence,' referencing ISIS material, authorities say.

The NYPD Bomb Squad found the device thrown at a protest yesterday was an improvised explosive, not a hoax or smoke bomb, and described it as a jar wrapped in black tape with nuts, bolts, screws, and a hobby fuse.
Police closed streets and ordered evacuations while a suspicious device was removed; FBI's Joint Terrorism Task Force and U.S. Attorney's Office are involved, with detainees in federal custody.
Follow-Up tests are underway on a second device, searches of suspects' homes and electronic devices are expected, and charges remain pending as federal interviewers continue questioning.
title: Explosives thrown near New York City mayor's residence investigated as 'ISIS-related terrorism'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the settlement as corporate malfeasance—using phrases like "illegal monopoly" and "terrorized" to stress coercion, spotlighting anecdotes and calling a divestiture of ~13 amphitheaters insufficient; it de-emphasizes precise penalty figures.

Right-Leaning pieces emphasize market stability, investor reaction and money—highlighting "Hundreds Of Millions Of Dollars," "Trump's DOJ" and dramatic cues such as "left fuming" to portray a practical avoidance of a Ticketmaster breakup, while de-emphasizing coercive anecdotes.
Center coverage parses legal mechanics and consumer claims; both sides note the tentative settlement and state AG objections, reflecting regulatory-versus-market priorities.
summary: At a Manhattan trial hearing, court records showed Live Nation Entertainment disclosed a proposed settlement with the U.S. Justice Department, still pending judicial approval.
The U.S. Justice Department and more than two dozen states sued in May 2024 seeking a Ticketmaster sale after Taylor Swift's 2022 Eras Tour ticket sale and alleged Live Nation abuses of dominance.
The settlement requires Live Nation to pay roughly $200 million in damages and divest up to 13 amphitheaters, while Ticketmaster must open parts of its technology to SeatGeek and Eventbrite.

New York Attorney General Letitia James and Massachusetts Attorney General Andrea Campbell said Monday their states will continue litigation, while Judge Arun Subramanian said, 'It shows absolute disrespect for the court, the jury and this entire process'.
Letitia James said the settlement 'fails to address the monopoly,' while Live Nation countered that the allegations are baseless.
title: Live Nation settles antitrust case with DOJ, avoids Ticketmaster breakup

bias: Left outlets emphasize a "dynastic succession" and describe the move as "defiant," stressing alleged illegitimacy, "hardline" continuity and humanitarian tolls, while right coverage foregrounds tags like "US‑sanctioned," "Regime," and "name not revealed," framing secrecy, securitized threat and endorsing Trump’s "unacceptable" critique and targeting rhetoric; rhetorically the left uses moralized, accusatory tones and casualty-focused language, the right relies on delegitimizing, security‑centered diction, center outlets favor attribution and economic fallout ; all agree on the Assembly’s selection and the IRGC’s pledge of allegiance.
summary: Late on Sunday, the Assembly of Experts announced Ayatollah Seyyed Mojtaba Khamenei , 56, as Iran's new Supreme Leader, Iranian state media and the president's press office reported.

After the Feb. 28 strikes that killed Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, former Supreme Leader, and about 40 other high-ranking Iranian officials, the United States and Israel intensified joint attacks inside Iran.

Despite his low public profile, Mojtaba Khamenei has long built influence as a gatekeeper and political broker across clerical, security, and political networks, despite never holding formal office and being sanctioned by the US Treasury in 2019.

Hardliners consolidated power, locking them firmly in control in Tehran and signaling Iran's ruling elite chose stability over experimentation; analysts say this expands the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps' authority and harshens domestic controls.
With direct threats looming, the United States and Israel warned successors would be targets, and President Donald Trump said a new leader would not last without US approval.
title: Iran names Khamenei's son, Mojtaba, as new supreme leader, state media report

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as human cost and policy failure—using words like "killed," "dies," "death toll," "grim new milestone" and labeling it an "unsanctioned war" driven by administration choices—while right-leaning outlets valorize sacrifice and leadership, foregrounding "fallen," "dignified transfer," "Hardest Thing I Have To Do," and operational language such as "Operation Epic Fury" that casts Iran as the "regime" and aggressor.
Center outlets report attack and timeline more plainly.

Dividing lines include ceremonial presidential optics, economic and infrastructure impacts, and whether the conflict is framed as avoidable or necessary response; all sides agree on seven deaths and Pentagon attribution.
summary: On March 8, 2026, U.S. Central Command announced a U.S. service member died from wounds sustained in an attack on U.S. troops in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia on March 1, 2026.
Following the Feb. 28 strikes on Iran, Iran responded with drone and missile strikes targeting Gulf countries and U.S. bases, including Prince Sultan Air Base.
The military is withholding the service member's identity until 24 hours after next-of-kin notification, and six Army Reserve members were killed in a March 1 drone strike in Kuwait.
The Pentagon said the death raises U.S. military fatalities to seven, and President Donald Trump attended a Saturday dignified transfer at Dover Air Force Base, Delaware.

Humanitarian agencies report the conflict has damaged civilian infrastructure, with the Iranian Red Crescent Society citing about 10,000 damaged structures and Lebanon reporting over a half-million displaced, while Bahrain accused Iran of striking a desalination plant.
title: US military says a US service member died of injuries sustained during an Iranian attack on US troops in Saudi Arabia

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the verdict as exposing elite abuse, foregrounding victims with phrases like "11 women’s testimony" and prosecutors' claim of "more than 60" alleged victims while highlighting "luxury" and opulence as tools of exploitation; right-leaning pieces stress scandal and consequence, emphasizing status with labels such as "brokers-to-the-stars" or "property hotshots" and casting doubt using terms like "twisted testimony" or dramatizing that they "learn fate.
Center outlets focus on legal facts and de-emphasize sensational claims.
All report the conviction, wealth ties and possible long sentences, reflecting divides over accountability versus punishment and skepticism.
summary: On Monday a Manhattan federal jury found Tal, Oren and Alon Alexander guilty of sex trafficking after deliberations. The decision followed a monthlong trial with testimony from nearly a dozen women.
Prosecutors allege the Alexander brothers conspired for more than a decade to drug and sexually assault women, recording assaults and plotting to sneak "party favors" aboard a cruise.

Eleven accusers testified during the one-month trial, with more than 30 witnesses describing assaults; Katie Moore said she was drugged and found naked with Alon Alexander, while Lindsey Acree felt paralyzed after less than half a glass of wine.
Besides the federal verdict, civil suits have proliferated, including one by Tracy Tutor, and Oren Alexander and Alon Alexander face separate Florida criminal cases, pledging not guilty.

The case attracted attention because the Alexander brothers were high-profile luxury brokers who sold a $240 million Midtown penthouse and prosecutors cited more than 60 women in documentary evidence.
title: Alexander brothers, top NY real estate brokers, convicted at sex trafficking trial

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the move as a moral human-rights condemnation, foregrounding labels like "wrongful detention" and "hostage diplomacy" and portraying Afghan actors as culpable, while right-leaning pieces stress punitive national-security responses—using terms such as "censuring," " travel ban," "detained Americans," "terrorist tactics," and combative verbs like "slams" and "dubs"—to justify hardline measures.
The center frames the designation as a policy tool, noting sanctions/export controls and legal criteria and naming detainees .
Crucial dividing lines are travel restrictions and motive-versus-punishment framing; all sides report the U.S. designation and accusation of "hostage diplomacy.".
summary: Secretary of State Marco Rubio designated Afghanistan as a state sponsor of wrongful detention on Monday, demanding Taliban authorities free two Americans and end hostage diplomacy.

The move responds to reports that the Taliban detained U.S. citizens to extract concessions, with the U.S. saying Mahmood Habibi vanished and Dennis Coyle is held without charges in near-solitary confinement.
The designation allows the Trump-era September executive order to be used, including sanctions, export controls, and a US$1 billion reward for information on Habibi, Rubio said.
Families of wrongfully detained Americans met with Rubio and praised his leadership, while U.S. diplomats accused the Taliban of 'hostage diplomacy,' pressuring for detainee releases.
The step follows a Feb. 27 designation of Iran and expands a new U.S. blacklist as Rubio questions the $1 billion in humanitarian aid for Afghanistan, the second country added to the list.
title: Rubio designates Afghanistan as ‘state sponsor of wrongful detention’

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as institutional failure and community harm—foregrounding "suing," "sewage spill," "negligence," and phrases like "massive sewage leak" to emphasize accountability and lingering public concern—while right-leaning pieces dramatize systemic risk with terms such as "barely escapes catastrophic-level," "America’s Largest," and "fragility of America’s aging infrastructure," invoking alarm about competence.
Center reporting is more granular—giving figures and class-action detail.

Dividing lines include spill volume, lifted advisories, and remediation specifics; all agree a pipeline collapse occurred, lawsuits followed, and cleanup is underway, highlighting environmental accountability vs. infrastructure concern.
summary: On Thursday, the Virginia Department of Health lifted the recreational-water advisory from Route 120 Chain Bridge to the Governor Harry W. Nice Memorial Bridge, and DC Water lifted its advisory near the district earlier this week.
Following the Jan. 19 collapse of a 72-inch section of the Potomac Interceptor, Virginia Department of Health issued an advisory on Feb. 13 after about 243 million gallons spilled into the river.

Virginia Department of Environmental Quality's late-February sampling found bacteria safe for swimming except a 4.7 miles stretch still under advisory, while Potomac Riverkeeper Network said E.coli is diminishing.

Local fishing operators report immediate business losses as Anthony Cubbage of Atomic Fishing Charters lost $5,000 in February while Maryland Health Department shellfish closure remains from the spill site to the Nice Bridge.

DC Water is managing repairs and diverting sewage into a canal as a temporary measure while contract crews remove boulders to replace the pipe, and advocates urge summer testing as agencies aggregate results.
title: Class Action Lawsuit Filed Against DC Water Following Potomac River Sewage Spill
topic_full: Washington DC Politics · District of Columbia

summary: In South Texas on March 9, ICE detained the family during check-ins, drawing bipartisan criticism, while the teenagers' release remains uncertain.
Amid ongoing immigration proceedings, the family had sought asylum and were complying with check-ins, according to supporters, before detention by ICE.
The teens were members of McAllen High School Mariachi Oro, which has visited the White House, performed at Carnegie Hall, and won eight state championships, according to local advocates.

Following the detentions, one brother was released Monday while the other four family members remain at a family detention center in Dilley, where Rep. Joaquin Castro visited and ICE did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
The case has prompted bipartisan criticism, with officials saying the family followed rules yet were torn apart by ICE amid border enforcement and immigration policy debates.
title: Two teen brothers in Texas mariachi band are released from ICE custody amid bipartisan criticism

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the story as moral outrage—using condemnatory verbs like "blew" and phrases such as "luxury cars and country club" and "defrauding" to spotlight egregious misuse of roughly $380,000 and a 21‑month sentence—while center coverage adopts neutral wording like "using about $380,000 on personal expenses" and "headed to federal prison ," adding context about prior "sexually explicit messages.
Right-Leaning coverage is de-emphasized in these analyses.

The pivotal divides are focus on sensational spending versus background misconduct; both sides nonetheless agree on the guilty plea, the amount and the sentence, reflecting deeper splits over elite corruption versus legal process.
summary: On Monday, former Missouri House Speaker John Diehl was sentenced to 21 months in federal prison after pleading guilty last year to misusing federal COVID-19 relief loans.

Diehl, a St. Louis-area attorney, obtained about $380,000 in federal loans for his law firm between 2020 and 2022 through an Economic Injury Disaster Loan and a loan modification acquired by false representations.

Prosecutors said he used more than half the funds to fund a defined benefit plan and paid for personal expenses including country club dues, pool maintenance, mortgage payments, and a Tesla, Audi, and Jeep.

The court also imposed a $50,000 fine and allowed Diehl to report to prison at a later date after his attorneys asked last month to avoid incarceration, citing repayment to the SBA, while prosecutors pushed for prison and argued repayment shouldn't warrant leniency.
Prosecutors framed Diehl's misconduct, citing his past privileges and public office, and said 'he knew better than to engage in the charged fraud scheme'.
title: Former Missouri House speaker sentenced to prison for COVID relief fraud

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes the law’s restrictiveness—using terms like "ban," "about 6 weeks," and clinical "cardiac activity" while foregrounding the governor's "misgivings" to stress urgent impacts on access; right-leaning coverage frames the move as protective and decisive—using "heartbeat," the bill's "Human Heartbeat Act" branding and citing opponents as an "attack on constitutional freedom," normalizing the restriction and stressing fetal protection; center outlets are more procedural, noting timing and a broader trend.
Both sides agree the law is signed, takes effect around six weeks, and will face imminent legal challenge, revealing deeper personhood-versus-autonomy divides.
summary: On Monday, Wyoming Gov. Mark Gordon signed HB 126 banning abortions after embryonic cardiac activity at about six weeks, including a medical exception for life or health risks.
Abortion access in recent years has been uneven after the state's sole clinic was set on fire in 2022, delaying its reopening and sometimes offering only medication abortion.
State health records show 625 abortions in 2024 and the Casper-based clinic provided 303 in 2025, while clinic staff began referring patients to out-of-state providers on Monday.
Burkhart added, 'We are prepared to challenge this ban in court and fight to protect reproductive rights, health and freedom in Wyoming', asserting the clinic's readiness for legal action.
The law's penalties include felony charges with up to five years in prison or $10,000 fines, amid state policy shifts since 2022 and 13 other states barring abortion at all stages.
title: Wyoming governor signs ban on abortions after about 6 weeks but cites misgivings

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the outcome as a failure of accountability—using phrases like "accountability matters" and "mass shooting" and foregrounding the victim, family grief, and moral outrage—while right-leaning pieces emphasize legal exculpation and brevity, citing "drops," "self-defense law," and "shooter" to portray the decision as justified by statute; rhetorically the left leans on emotive, victim-centered language, the right on procedural, law-focused language.
The pivotal divide is over Missouri’s self-defense rules and whether a reduced charge signals leniency or necessary caution; center coverage is more detail-driven, listing original charges.
Both sides note prosecutors cited self-defense complexity.
summary: On March 9, Jackson County judge sentenced Dominic Miller to two years in the Missouri Department of Corrections after he pleaded guilty to unlawful use of a weapon, with credit for time served.
At the Kansas City Chiefs rally outside Union Station, Missouri, on Feb. 14, 2024, gunfire erupted as the event ended, killing Lisa Lopez-Galvan while she attended with her family.

Court records show Dominic Miller was among at least six shooters, while investigators reported 12 people who brandished firearms, including two AR-style rifles, and a 15-year-old shooter struck Miller.
Lyndell Mays is scheduled to stand trial next year, and families of rally attendees later filed a negligence lawsuit after at least 23 people were shot and at least 12 injured fleeing the violence.
title: Prosecutor drops murder charge against shooter at Chiefs rally, citing state's self-defense laws

bias: Left outlets emphasize skepticism and subservience—framing Trump's 'mutual' claim as capitulation with charged words like 'totally caved' and highlighting economic motives , while right outlets emphasize decisive coordination and triumph—repeating 'mutual' as allied solidarity and using militaristic terms like 'destroyed' to signal success.

Rhetorically, the left uses accusatory, moralizing language; the right uses declarative, triumphalist tones; center outlets attribute quotes neutrally and stress U.S. authority, while left de-emphasized Iranian vows to fight long-term.
Dividing lines include agency, motive and conflict duration; all report the 'mutual' claim and continued fighting.
summary: U.S. President Donald Trump said ending the war with Iran will be a mutual decision made with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
Trump stated, "I think it’s mutual... a little bit. We’ve been talking. I’ll make a decision at the right time, but everything’s going to be taken into account".
The U.S. will only withdraw from Iran when Israel decides it is time to end the conflict.
Trump dismissed the idea that Israel might continue its campaign against Iran after U.S. withdrawal, saying, "I don’t think it’s going to be necessary".
title: Trump says ‘mutual’ decision with Israel will end Iran war

bias: Left-Leaning coverage casts ChatGPT as an active wrongdoer, using charged phrases like "collaborator, trusted confidant" and "helped the killer" to emphasize moral culpability and human impact , while right-leaning pieces de-emphasize court specifics and fatalities, framing the "suing" more transactionally—labeling the firm the "ChatGPT-maker" and using terms like "critically injured" without reproducing the lawsuit's "specific knowledge" claim.
Center coverage instead focuses on legal mechanics—"specific knowledge" and "despite employee warnings.
The dividing lines are AI moral agency and alleged internal warnings; both sides agree a lawsuit was filed and the shooter allegedly used ChatGPT.
summary: On Monday, the family of Maya Gebala filed a B.C. Supreme Court lawsuit against OpenAI over the Tumbler Ridge shooting, seeking accountability for Maya, her sister Dahlia, and her mother Cia Edmonds.

Internal moderation records show about 12 OpenAI employees flagged posts as an 'imminent risk' and recommended informing law enforcement, but leadership allegedly ignored these warnings, failed to detect a second account, and did not implement age verification.

Shot three times, Maya Gebala was left with catastrophic brain injury and remains at BC Children's Hospital where doctors removed her breathing tube on March 7; eight people were killed in the Feb. 10 attack.
After the attack, OpenAI ultimately alerted the RCMP but has not been served in the suit and has not responded, while the OpenAI Foundation also remains silent, filings show.
The suit alleges the GPT-4o model was 'intentionally designed to foster psychological dependency' and plaintiffs say it was rushed to market prioritizing engagement over safety.
title: Family Sues OpenAI over Mass Shooting in Tumbler Ridge, B.C.

summary: On Monday, Franklin County Magistrate Jennifer D. Hunt issued an injunction blocking the Browns from accessing Ohio unclaimed funds until the lawsuit is resolved.

Under House Bill 96, the Ohio General Assembly and Gov. Mike DeWine created a Sports and Culture Facilities Fund using a $3.7 billion pool of unclaimed taxpayer money, which plaintiffs argue violates the Ohio Constitution by taking private property without due process or proper 'public use'.
Her written order said the state failed to give proper notice to taxpayers and concluded the use did not qualify as a 'public use' under the Ohio Constitution.
The ruling pauses transfers scheduled to begin Jan. 1, while construction at the Brook Park site has already started, and lawyers may object as a judge considers the magistrate's decision.
The plaintiffs argue that Ohio's law allowing claims on funds unclaimed for at least 10 years is challenged, with the state's top lawyer saying the plan is legally sound.
title: Judge pauses Ohio’s plan to fund new Browns stadium with unclaimed funds

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the verdict with legal verbs like "acquits" and couches the charge as "accused of lying," noting the "deadly" 2017 crash that "killed 16" service members; right-leaning headlines also use "acquits" and "lying" yet stress individual vindication and de-emphasize the casualty count; center pieces add context—name , an eight-day trial, flight route and that it "broke apart in midair.
The key divides are whether coverage foregrounds fatalities or exoneration and how terms like "lying" and "obstruction of justice " are deployed; all report the acquittal and link it to the crash.
summary: On Thursday, James Michael Fisher, former lead propulsion engineer, was acquitted after an eight-day trial in Greenville, Mississippi federal court.
A federal grand jury accused Fisher of lying about about 30 inspection changes approved by engineers at the Georgia base, undermining trust, as prosecutors alleged.
The KC-130T's fractured blade caused the aircraft to break apart, killing fifteen Marines and one Navy corpsman, with debris spread across two to three miles near Itta Bena, Mississippi.

The military grounded some C-130s and ordered inspections after the crash, while Steve Farese, Fisher's defense lawyer, said 'Nobody did it intentionally' and blamed others for inspection changes in Brazil.
So far, the trial has left open core questions about responsibility, as trial testimony provided no clear explanation and witnesses said there were 10 different ways the blade could have been missed.
title: Mississippi jury acquits engineer accused of lying about 2017 military plane crash

bias: Left outlets frame Kiley’s exit as performative and cynical—using phrases like "all for show," "tear into," and "supposed" and foregrounding activist critiques that he still backs GOP leaders—while right outlets stress tangible loss and urgency, deploying language such as "another," "immediately," and that it "cut even thinner" the "slim majority.
Center coverage emphasizes electoral calculus—"tough reelection battle," "dealt new blow"—and notes the tariff vote and whether he will "caucus.
The key divides are motive and caucusing; all agree the switch is immediate and pressures the GOP majority, reflecting deeper ideological and strategic rifts.
summary: On Monday, Rep. Kevin Kiley, U.S. Representative from California, said he was immediately leaving the Republican Party and will serve as an independent for the remainder of his term.
Earlier this month, Kiley announced he would run in California's 6th Congressional District after redistricting created a tougher reelection map, choosing to face Democratic challengers.

Kiley said he did not notify GOP leadership before his departure but spoke with House Speaker Mike Johnson and asked the House clerk to reflect his change to independent while caucusing with Republicans.
With his move, the House majority shifts to 217-214 with one independent, after Kiley announced on Friday he would run in a Democratic-leaning district without party affiliation.

The move illustrates how the redistricting war that began in Texas at President Donald Trump's urging left some incumbents scrambling in states like California, prompting strategic shifts in recent weeks.
title: GOP lawmaker says he’s leaving the Republican Party and will serve as an independent

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames Trump as "downplaying" a meaningful rift—using skeptical quotes around "philosophically" and emphasizing internal hesitation—while right-leaning pieces minimize discord, stressing Vance was "quite enthusiastic" and using mitigating terms like "acknowledged" and "maybe less enthusiastic" to portray unity; center outlets are more neutral, reporting phrases such as "philosophically a little bit different" and "no choice" without partisan spin.

The pivotal divide is whether these remarks signal a substantive split or routine policy nuance; all sides nevertheless report Trump defended the strikes as "necessary" and say officials ultimately concurred.
summary: President Donald Trump at Trump National Doral said Vice President J.D. Vance was 'philosophically a little bit different than me' but denied a formal disagreement, saying 'We get along very well on this'.
Amid rising intra-party tension, the war in Iran has stoked Republican tension over its fit with the 'America First' movement, which Vance has embodied as a top 2028 contender.

Vance has remained publicly supportive since the strikes and attended Dover Air Force Base for Sgt. Benjamin Pennington's dignified transfer, while telling The Washington Post there was 'no chance' of a drawn-out war.

The administration has issued conflicting messages about war duration, with Trump describing it as able to run 'as long as necessary' and later calling it a 'short-term excursion' amid economic turmoil.
Trump's hawkish record, including ordering the drone strike that killed Qassem Soleimani, widely seen as escalating tensions, and ending the Iran nuclear deal, shapes current policy choices.
title: Trump says Vance was ‘philosophically’ different on Iran while downplaying split

bias: Left coverage frames the president’s post as menacing and escalatory—using verbs like "threatens" and phrases such as "Death, Fire, and Fury" and "promises to bomb" to portray reckless bellicosity; right coverage frames identical language as decisive deterrence, invoking "stern warning," "20 times harder," "retaliation," "evil terrorists," and claims about "easily destroyable" targets to justify force.
Center outlets attribute quotes and note a possible quick end or domestic energy impacts , de-emphasized on the right.
All agree a threat was issued, the Strait’s strategic role matters, and regional tensions are central, exposing ideological security and cultural divides.
summary: Monday evening, Donald Trump posted on Truth Social a warning that 'If Iran does anything that stops the flow of Oil within the Strait of Hormuz, they will be hit by the United States of America TWENTY TIMES HARDER than they have been hit thus far'.
Amid reports of Iran rallying behind Mojtaba Khamenei, Trump authorized unprecedented U.S. strikes last week, including a reported strike that destroyed an elementary school in Iran killing 175.
The Strait of Hormuz handles one-fifth of the world's oil supply, and Trump said U.S. attacks could rise sharply if Iran blocked tanker traffic after serious damage to Iran's air force and navy.

Brent crude futures jumped about 7% to their highest since 2022, while a Reuters/Ipsos poll released Monday found 67% expect gas prices to rise and Iran's Revolutionary Guards warned they would block oil exports if attacks continue.

After speaking with Russian President Vladimir Putin, Trump said the U.S. administration will waive oil-related sanctions on some countries, while options include releasing oil from strategic reserves amid at least 1,332 Iranian civilian deaths.
title: Trump warns Iran of response '20 times harder' if Strait of Hormuz blocked

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes victim-focused wrongdoing and secrecy—using terms like "secluded" and "convicted sex offender" and framing the search as exposing alleged sex trafficking—while right-leaning pieces foreground claims of institutional interference, personalizing accusations with phrases such as "Comey’s daughter" and verbs like "halted" and "gutted," implying corruption.
Rhetorically the left uses moralized, evocative language; the right uses accusatory, scandal-driven framing.
Centers de-emphasize partisan claims and instead detail procedures, naming AG Raúl Torrez and a 2019 email.
Both sides agree a search occurred and link the property to Epstein, highlighting deeper ideological divides over accountability and institutional trust.
summary: On Monday, NMDOJ searched Zorro Ranch at the direction of Attorney General Raúl Torrez, with assistance from the New Mexico State Police and Sandoval County Sheriff's Office.
The search follows a criminal investigation announced February 19 that Public Lands Commissioner Stephanie Garcia Richard requested after reviewing a 2019 email.
An anonymous 2019 email alleged two girls were killed and buried near the ranch about 30 miles southeast of Santa Fe, seeking one bitcoin for videos, according to accounts tied to the email.
Officials asked anyone with information to submit tips and warned the public to avoid the ranch and not to fly drones near the scene, pledging to support survivors and keep the public informed.
Andrea Romero, New Mexico State Representative, warned the state's 2019 closure risked evidence at the 7,500-plus-acre property , which sold on Aug. 22, 2023.
title: New Mexico prosecutors launch search of Jeffrey Epstein’s secluded former Zorro Ranch

bias: Left coverage emphasizes an institutional threat, portraying the administration as mounting a "systematic effort" to "diminish the power" of federal judges and spotlighting vitriolic rhetoric—terms like "rogue," "crooked," "lunatic"—to characterize attacks on the judiciary.
Right coverage frames the same moves as a legitimate push to overcome rulings "slowing" an immigration "agenda," stressing executive prerogative.
Center coverage focuses on procedural legal requests and who could be affected, warning the plan could "expose thousands" to deportation.
All agree there is a Supreme Court appeal and major human consequences, reflecting deeper ideological splits over judicial authority and immigration.
summary: On Monday, the U.S. Justice Department asked the U.S. Supreme Court to clear the way for faster termination of migrants' legal protections and bar courts from questioning Department of Homeland Security decisions.
The Trump administration has repeatedly filed emergency petitions, with 31 requests since February 2025, 97% claiming lower-court judges improperly interfere and 68% denying judges' review authority.
DHS has ended protections for about 600,000 Venezuelans and appealed a ruling on Syrians last month, while planning to challenge a decision affecting about 350,000 Haitians.

Immediate consequences could include exposing migrants to deportation, as attorneys for Haitian migrants warn 'people will almost certainly die' if the program ends while the U.S. Justice Department appeals a ruling affecting about 350,000 Haitians.
The Supreme Court's upcoming April 1 argument comes as more than 175 former judges urge allowing the normal appeals process to play out rather than resolving emergency petitions.
title: Trump administration criticizes court rulings slowing immigration agenda in Supreme Court appeal

bias: Left outlets frame the crisis as systemic and humanitarian, emphasizing infrastructure harm with phrases like "puts at risk," portraying U.S. action as a "war of choice" and Iran's aim as an "intolerable economic cost," while right outlets securitize it—using "threatens global oil supply," "come under fire," and "severely disrupted"—blaming Iran and stressing supply shocks .
Rhetorically, left uses public‑welfare alarm, right uses threat‑language; the center foregrounds figures and domestic inflation impacts.
Disputes over responsibility and Hormuz’s status are pivotal; all agree flows are disrupted and prices have jumped, reflecting broader ideological and geopolitical priorities.
summary: This past week, the Iran war has put pipelines, refineries and shipping terminals at risk, leading state-owned QatarEnergy to shut a terminal and Iraq to suspend 1.5 million barrels per day at Rumaila and West Qurna.

Analysts say Iran aimed at oil storage and reroute points like Fujairah oil terminal, United Arab Emirates and Kharg Island, Iran, forcing shutdowns and hitting pipelines that avoid the Strait of Hormuz.
At Saudi Aramco’s Ras Tanura, the port and refinery, operations were temporarily halted after a drone caused a fire, while Brent crude rose from $72.97 to almost $103 on Monday.

Restart timelines indicate that Iraq and other Gulf oil fields have cut output as onshore storage fills, and experts warn some facilities will take weeks or months to resume, industry analysts including Torbjorn Soltvedt said.
The Strait of Hormuz matters because it is effectively closed to some traffic and channels about 20% of global oil and LNG, while Al Basra Oil Terminal, Iraq exports oil worth 80% of Iraq’s GDP.
title: Iran war puts at risk key pipelines, terminals and refineries that supply the world with oil and gas

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the departure as a partisan opportunity—using phrasing like "giving liberals chance to expand majority" to spotlight gains—while right-leaning pieces foreground the justice ’s ideological label and present the vacancy as a zero-sum loss or consequence, even noting she was unopposed in 2017; left uses outcome-focused language, right uses label-first, alarmed framing.
Center coverage is distinct in emphasizing personal reasons , tenure and procedural context.
Both sides concur on the basic developments and stakes for redistricting, unions, and schools, reflecting deeper ideological divides.
summary: Justice Annette Ziegler announced Monday in Milwaukee, Wisconsin she will not seek reelection to the Wisconsin Supreme Court in 2027 and will finish her current term, with the election scheduled for April 6, 2027.

Part of the court's conservative wing, Ziegler said, 'After three decades on the bench, now is the right time for me to step away to spend more time with my husband, kids and grandkids' after liberals gained majority earlier this year.
A former Washington County judge, Ziegler was first elected to the Wisconsin Supreme Court in 2007 with 59 percent and served two terms as chief justice.

There is another election on April 7 for the open seat caused by conservative Justice Rebecca Bradley's decision not to run for reelection, which could impact court control if liberals win this year, increasing their majority to 5-2.
Recent races drew national attention as liberals have struck down abortion laws and could expand their majority in upcoming years amid high spending by outside actors.
title: Conservative Annette Ziegler announces retirement from Wisconsin Supreme Court

bias: Left-Leaning outlets frame the resignation as a voluntary "mistake" following "self-disclosure," humanizing the leader.
Conversely, right-leaning coverage portrays the scandal as one that "forces" resignation, emphasizing "accountability" and "revealing" misconduct.
While the right highlights the misuse of "public resources," the left de-emphasizes this, focusing on the personal "reporting" of the error.
Center-Leaning reports provide context on the "ongoing investigation" and clarify his tenure began in January 2024.
Both sides agree on the core "inappropriate relationship," yet their portrayals reflect a broader ideological divide between institutional protection and critiques of academic elites.
summary: Updated March 9, 2026: Walter "Ted" Carter Jr., president of The Ohio State University, resigned and the board accepted his resignation after a weekend executive session, disclosing an inappropriate relationship.
In the update on March 9, Carter told trustees he had an inappropriate relationship with someone seeking public resources and allowed inappropriate access to Ohio State leadership.
Before Ohio State, Carter's contract was set to run through Dec. 31, 2028, and he previously led the University of Nebraska system.
The trustees said they will share a transition plan later this week and no interim president has been announced.

Amid ongoing initiatives, Carter's resignation may impact the university's strategic priorities, including the Education for Citizenship 2035 plan, as the campus community awaits further details soon.
title: Ohio State University's president resigns after reporting 'inappropriate relationship'

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the event as contextualized tragedy—using terms like "prank gone wrong," humanizing the teens and highlighting the family's plea for "charges dropped"—while right-leaning coverage foregrounds law-and-order, using phrases such as "charged students," "horribly wrong," and spotlighting community fundraising to demand accountability.
Center outlets present a matter-of-fact "prank that turned deadly," noting the 18‑year‑old driver’s arrest and 40‑year‑old teacher Jason Hughes's death.

The wife's request to drop charges, intent versus culpability, and inconsistent charging counts are pivotal dividing lines; all agree on the death, arrests, and communal mourning, reflecting deeper ideological priorities.
summary: On March 6, Jason Hughes, a 40-year-old math teacher and golf coach at North Hall High School, was struck outside his home and later died at a hospital.
The prank, known locally as rolling, followed a Hall County School District warning that five high school students could face charges for prom-season TP-ing.

The Hall County Sheriff's Office arrested on Friday and charged Jayden Ryan Wallace with first-degree vehicular homicide and reckless driving; four others face trespass and littering charges and were released on bond on Friday.

Family members asked that charges be dropped to avoid harming the teens' futures, calling the death a terrible tragedy, while community memorials and an online fundraiser raised more than $200,000 as of Monday.

A preliminary hearing on April 1 is scheduled as prosecutors and investigators continue to review the incident; Lee Darragh, Hall County District Attorney, said, 'It is much too early in this process. I’ll not be commenting until the closure of the case.
title: Family of Teacher Killed During Prank Wants Charges Against Students Dropped

bias: Left-Leaning coverage emphasizes loss and professionalism—framing MacFarlane as "hard-charging" and "respected" and casting exits as post-merger "shakeups"—while right-leaning pieces portray his move as ideological defection, highlighting "sudden" departure, quoting "some independence," and ridiculing him with labels like "Crazy-Ex Scotty" and "January 6-Obsessed"; center outlets lean factual but sometimes use alarmist words like "shocking" or "chaos.

Pivotal dividing lines are Bari Weiss' hiring/ownership and MacFarlane’s Jan. 6 beat, which trigger opposing portrayals; all sides agree he’s leaving, cited "independence," and that departures followed management change.
summary: Scott MacFarlane, who worked at CBS News since 2021, has announced his exit from the network, stating he is looking forward to some independence.
Paramount Skydance CEO David Ellison appointed The Free Press co-founder Bari Weiss as CBS News editor-in-chief in October after the merger.
Some have criticized Weiss and Ellison for trying to appease the Trump administration and moving CBS News into a more MAGA-friendly network.
title: CBS Justice Correspondent Leaving Network: ‘I Look Forward to some Independence’

bias: Left coverage frames the return as exclusive and questions access—using language like "won't be open to everyone"—and de-emphasizes celebratory spin; right coverage frames it as a patriotic restoration, calling Mount Rushmore "iconic" and proclaiming "GREAT NEWS," highlighting state officials' praise.

Center reporting sits between them, detailing July 3 timing, a reported five‑year hiatus, ticketing via "public lottery," and flagging expert warnings of an "unprecedented snow drought" and choices made "despite wildfire risk," noting NPS "greenlights" the plan and that "Trump may attend.
All agree NPS announced fireworks for the 250th, exposing cultural versus safety and access divides.
summary: On Monday, March 9, the National Park Service announced fireworks will return to Mount Rushmore National Memorial on July 3, 2026, with public tickets allocated via a Recreation.gov lottery from April 8 to 12.

Once suspended from 2009 through 2019 over wildfire concerns, fireworks resumed in 2020 after Kristi Noem, then-South Dakota governor, lobbied under President Donald Trump 's administration; permit requests were denied in 2021 and 2022 by the Biden administration.

Data show mounting wildfire risk as Pennington County faces severe drought and Western states record lowest snowpack in decades, while NPS studies linked past fireworks to over 20 wildfires and a 60% wildfire chance.
Officials said ticketed attendees must apply via Recreation.gov from April 8 to 12 for the July 3 reopening, with results announced on April 14.

Monday's announcement omitted wildfire risk or fire-prevention plans while Indigenous tribes oppose fireworks in the sacred Black Hills; South Dakota Gov. Larry Rhoden said, 'In just a few short months, our nation will throw the biggest birthday party ever for the United States of America.
title: NPS announces return of Mount Rushmore July 4th fireworks

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the president's exhortation as coercive and reckless—using terms like 'bully' and noting a 'spike' in oil prices and danger to crews—while right-leaning pieces cast the same 'show some guts' line as tough, patriotic leadership, lauding 'Operation Epic Fury' and saying crews have 'nothing to be afraid of.' Center reports 'urges' and emphasizes market moves—Brent briefly topped $100 per barrel—more than partisan framing.

Pivotal divides include naming/celebrating the military operation and whether rhetoric endangers or reassures sailors; both sides agree he urged transit and linked it to oil‑price risk, reflecting wider hawkish vs. safety‑first priorities.
summary: On Monday, President Donald Trump urged oil transportation companies and foreign-flag tanker crews to resume transits through the Strait of Hormuz as global oil prices surged over $100 per barrel.
Regional drone and rocket fire forced shipping to largely halt last week after U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iranian military installations, with large tanker anchorages forming.
The Strait of Hormuz normally handles one fifth of global oil and over 90 percent of its flow supplies Asian economies, led by China, while U.S.-flagged tankers account for about 0.6 percent.

Analysts warned freight rates hit record highs on Friday due to geopolitical risks, while VLCC earnings reach $480,000 daily and the G7 economies' midday move cut oil prices about 20 percent on Monday.

With about 150 launchers left, Iran has threatened to set tankers 'on fire,' and the IMO reports at least seven seafarers killed, while additional U.S. naval assets may escort ships after one passed through Sunday with no problem.
title: Trump: Oil tanker crews must ‘show some guts,’ sail through Straight of Hormuz

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the ruling as accountability and remembrance, using emotive words like "deadly," "catastrophic," "preserve" and noting "fully reopening"; right-leaning pieces present the decision tersely, foregrounding a judge's "orders" and an AP dateline to emphasize authority; center reporting de-emphasizes rhetoric, favoring procedural terms such as "swept away" and noting the suit behind the order.
A pivotal divide is whether owners "must not alter or demolish" the cabins—phrasing stressed on left/center and de-emphasized on the right.
All sides agree a Texas judge ordered preservation after a lawsuit tied to floods that swept away and killed 25 girls and two counselors.
summary: A Texas judge ordered Camp Mystic to preserve damaged cabins and grounds hit by last year's catastrophic floods that killed 25 girls and two counselors.
The order follows a lawsuit by the family of 8-year-old Cile Steward, who was swept away in the flood and whose body has not been recovered.
Camp Mystic's owners were ordered not to alter or demolish the cabins where campers were housed during the floods, and not to use the portion of the camp closest to the Guadalupe River.
title: Texas judge orders Camp Mystic to preserve damaged grounds hit by last year's deadly flood
Syria Blames Hizbollah for Artillery Strikes Near Damascus

bias: Left-Leaning narratives frame the Iranian president’s apology as evidence of internal "fracturing," highlighting a divide between "pragmatic" and "hardline" factions.
In contrast, right-leaning outlets portray the gesture as "humiliating" and "degrading," emphasizing nationalist demands to "take the microphone away.
While the left focuses on diplomatic shifts, the right prioritizes perceived regime weakness, even suggesting the president apologize domestically.
Center-Leaning coverage employs sensationalist verbs like "slammed" and "pillory" while including broader regional context.
Both perspectives agree the apology was "weak" and "unacceptable" to critics, illustrating deep ideological divides over international conciliation versus national strength.
summary: On Saturday, March 7, 2026, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologised on state media, saying 'I personally apologize to neighbouring countries that were affected by Iran 's actions', but omitted the apology on social media.
Amid intense external strikes, the interim council including Pezeshkian agreed to suspend attacks on nearby states, prompting de-escalation gestures amid a leadership vacuum after Khamenei's death.
Hardline cleric Hamid Rasai called Pezeshkian's stance 'unprofessional, weak and unacceptable', and Raja News described it as 'begging diplomacy', urging to 'take the microphone away' from him.

The episode has revealed visible fractures within Iran's ruling elite as hardliners, including judiciary chief Ayatollah Gholamhossein Mohseni-Ejei, pushed retaliatory strikes, and senior ayatollahs urged the Assembly of Experts to speed leadership selection.

Meanwhile, the regional war has spilled into neighbouring states and markets as U.S.-Israeli strikes killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians, Iranian attacks killed 10 in Israel and six U.S. service members, and Gulf output cuts disrupted flights.
title: Israel, Iran trade more blows, Iranian president draws criticism at home for apology

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames detainees as "pro‑Palestinian activists" organizing a "humanitarian flotilla" and emphasizes that authorities "detained" them—conveying solidarity and rights-focused concern—while right-leaning pieces de-emphasize that portrayal, calling the mission a "phantom humanitarian mission" and flagging "irregularities," adopting a skeptical, accusatory tone.
Left uses legitimizing labels and empathetic diction; right uses delegitimizing, judgmental language.
Pivotal dividing lines are the flotilla's legitimacy, organizers' stated aims, and the reported financial inquiry.
Center reports use neutral verbs and affiliation labels.
Both sides agree detentions occurred and tensions mounted, reflecting deeper ideological splits over activism versus security.
summary: On Friday, Tunisian authorities detained several pro-Palestinian activists amid a probe by Tunisia 's National Guard financial crimes unit into donation funds for a planned flotilla.
Authorities banned a planned event Thursday in Tunis expected to host Thiago Ávila, Greta Thunberg and Seif Abu Koshk, while earlier this week security forces blocked a ceremony at Sidi Bou Said.
Organizers say the new flotilla will include more than 1,000 activists and sail from Spain, Tunisia and Italy with a land convoy expected to attract thousands more.

Global Sumud Flotilla organizers accused authorities of targeting activists and called for their immediate release; Tunisian authorities did not comment, and Ávila was detained Friday at Brussels Airport.

The move occurs amid Gaza's ongoing humanitarian crisis as Israel suspended more than two dozen humanitarian organizations and the United Nations reports large aid stockpiles since the October ceasefire.
title: Tunisian authorities detain pro-Palestinian activists preparing new Gaza aid flotilla

bias: Left-Leaning coverage frames the arrival as escalation—using "Huge," highlighting a single B-52 and domestic politics and aligning with the MoD's "specific defensive operations" phrasing—while right-leaning pieces deploy hawkish, militarized terms like "Devastating," "Major Strike," "Trump Promises" and stress nuclear-capable B-52s, de-emphasizing the MoD's defensive wording; center outlets are more technical, noting at least three B-1B Lancers, saying "strikes intensify" and that MPs "raise fears.
Key divides are aircraft type/number, MoD framing, and the politicized linkage to imminent offensive action; all agree long-range bombers landed amid Iran tensions.
summary: On March 9, three B-52H Stratofortress bombers from Minot Air Force Base landed at RAF Fairford , joining U.S. B-1B Lancer bombers to sustain strikes on Iran.
The U.K. MoD confirmed Saturday that U.S. forces had begun using RAF Fairford for 'specific defensive operations' after Starmer authorized the request on March 1 for a 'limited defensive purpose'.

B-1B Lancers first arrived the evening of March 6 with more on March 7, while CENTCOM Commander Adm. Brad Cooper said nearly 200 bomber strikes hit Iran in 72 hours; the B-1B carries 24 missiles internally and the B-52 up to 70,000 pounds of weapons.

Basing heavy bombers at RAF Fairford significantly shortens mission turnaround compared with flights from the continental United States, while Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth warned on March 4 that strikes were 'about to surge dramatically' and shifting to gravity bombs.

This deployment marks the first time B-52s have appeared in the U.K. during this conflict, while local residents and plane-spotters caused roadblocks that Gloucestershire Police managed around RAF Fairford.
title: US Bombers Deploy to RAF Fairford to Sustain Strikes on Iran

bias: Left-Leaning pieces frame the poll as a surprising, "stunning" or "wild" reversal that alarms Democrats—even noting the 1,000‑sample, ±3.1‑point margin—while right-leaning coverage revels and ridicules, using "hilarious," "damning," and "wake up call" to tout ICE's comparative popularity, a reported 27‑point border advantage, and alleged "media" bias.
Rhetorically the left adopts startled, cautionary tones; the right adopts derisive, triumphant language; centrist accounts de-emphasize ICE and stress the measured 44% job‑approval figure.
Both sides, however, pivot on the same poll result, reflecting deeper divides over immigration, media trust, and partisan competence.
summary: A new NBC News poll ending on Tuesday, March 10, 2026, shows President Donald Trump more popular than Kamala Harris at 34%-51% and Gavin Newsom at 27%-45%.
The survey of 1,000 registered voters, which had a 3.1% margin of error and was conducted February 27–March 3, captures sentiment in the opening months of the 2026 midterm year.
The poll found 44% approve and 54% disapprove of President Donald Trump's job approval, with net approval shifting from-12 to-10.
A NBC News poll shows the GOP improved to 37%-51% compared with Democrats' 30%-52%, with 50% of voters preferring Democrats to control Congress after the midterms.
Data from the favorability list show Pope Leo XIV ranked high, while Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Artificial Intelligence were among the least popular.
title: Trump more popular than Harris, Newsom: Survey

summary: This year, Enterprise Community Partners reported 39,880 affordable units across 461 shovel-ready developments in California stalled for lack of public funding, with many waiting over a year after applying in February.
After years of permitting reform, developers now clear local approvals but face financing bottlenecks from relying on two to six public funding sources with separate timelines.

The Terner Center for Housing Innovation at UC Berkeley reports that each additional funding source delays projects by four months and adds $20,460 per unit, with 1,198 units waiting on final funding, including the Modesto project on East Morris Avenue.
California Housing and Community Development says at least $1.8 billion should be available this year while lawmakers consider a $10 billion affordable housing bond for 2026.
The Newsom administration proposes funding, which could benefit 432 households, and plans a California Housing and Homelessness Agency to speed project delivery.
title: California has 40,000 affordable housing units ready to break ground. One setback is holding them up
